svg.font-family: -apple-system, Segoe UI, Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif

g.text: The Four Layers of an ATDD Extension
How ATDD packages rules, runtimes, and validator code — and shares runtimes across extensions.

svg.text: Extension = the use-case package · Workspace Provider = the shared runtime · Instance = the runtime materialized for a run
· Implementation = the code that enforces the rule. The runtime is referenced by id + contract version, never copied.

g.text: 1. The big picture — the extension owns its rule; the runtime is shared
• use-case package — install / version / remove as a unit
…and it CONTAINS the implementation that enforces the rule:
but it RUNS in a workspace instance →
• reusable, domain-agnostic — NOT inside the extension
• the provider resolved locally for a run
Encapsulation, with one twist: the extension contains its implementation — but the runtime is a shared provider, referenced by id + contract, not nested inside it.
The provider materializes into an instance where the implementation runs. That separation is the whole point — one runtime, many extensions.
the use case is ONE extension · the runtime is a SHARED provider · the instance is generated
Like an npm package or a
Like a Docker image or a
4. Runtime flow — from install to violation
extension metadata — never on the runtime.
A runtime used by more than one extension
is a first-class provider from day one —
referenced by id + contract, never copied.

svg.text: ATDD Extensions · github.com/afokapu/atdd-extensions — the four-layer model: Extension → Workspace Provider → Workspace Instance → Implementation
